records.ety: [OE. sedicioun, OF. sedition, F. sdition, fr. L. seditio, originally, a going aside; hence, an insurrectionary separation; pref. se-, sed-, aside + itio a going, fr. ire, itum, to go. Cf. Issue.]

defs: The raising of commotion in a state, not amounting to insurrection; conduct tending to treason, but without an overt act; excitement of discontent against the government, or of resistance to lawful authority.
quotes: In soothing them, we nourish 'gainst our senate The cockle of rebellion, insolence, sedition.
Noisy demagogues who had been accused of sedition.
Now the works of the flesh are manifest, . . . emulations, wrath, strife, seditions, heresies.
